coredata.dc:title: Intramolecular charge transfer mediated dual-emission in multi-doped graphene quantum dots for anti-counterfeiting via liquid secret key
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Changxing
Li, Xiameng
Li, Tingting
Wang, Yiru
Guo, Guoqiang
Tang, Siyuan
Gu, Chenjie
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Multiple emission GQDs have always been the focus of researchers due to the unique emission characteristics and high-level application capabilities. Nevertheless, the most of GQDs with double emissions are formed by different chemical reactions, and the realization of double emission by adjusting the solvent environment is rarely reported. Here, the multi-doped and double-peak emissive GQDs (MD-GQDs) have been synthesized via one pot hydrothermal synthesis and re-dispersed in solvents with different polarity. Systematic characterizations of optical property indicate that the solvent polarity related double-peak emission photoluminescence is ascribed to the existence of multi-emissive centers in MD-GQDs from aromatic CO bond and surface/edge state (C–N/C–F). Furthermore, the double emission fluorescent mechanism of MD-GQDs has been proposed due to the electrostatic interaction between MD-GQDs and solvent molecules, which induces intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect in MD-GQDs. Finally, based on the unique solvent-induced discoloration behavior of MD-GQDs, a multi-mode anti-counterfeiting application based on organic solution as the secret key with easy operation and high visual effect is developed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MD-GQDs
Intramolecular charge transfer
Double-peak emission
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Intramolecular charge transfer mediated dual-emission in MD-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: K C Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084 ), and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University . The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the MD-GQDs. Inset: high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of MD-GQDs. (b) Particle size distribution measured by TEM. (c) AFM image of MD-GQDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of MD-GQDs. High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (e), N 1s (f), O 1s (g), P 2p (h) and F 1s (i).

(a) UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of MD-GQDs. The inset is the optical image under daylight (left) and a 365 nm illumination (right). (b) Fluorescence spectra of MD-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Excitation-emission matrix graph map for MD-GQDs. The fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength of MD-GQDs under (d) pH = 1–14 and (e) different ionic strengths of NaCl aqueous solution (0–2 mol/L). (f) Long-term fluorescence stability of MD-GQDs in water.

Two-dimensional excitation-emission plots of MD-GQDs in different solvents: water (a), formamide (b), ethanol (c), DMSO (d), DMF (e) and toluene (f). (g–l) The corresponding fluorescence emission spectra of MD-GQDs in different solvents.

(a–c) 3D fluorescence spectra and (d–e) UV–Vis absorption spectra of MD-GQDs dispersed in water solvent with varying volume ratios of DMSO (DMSO: water). (f) Emission wavelength as a function of the solvent polarity parameter, ET N; the solid line is a linear fit of the data. (g) Overall fluorescence schematic diagram of the MD-GQDs. Time-resolved PL spectra of MD-GQDs dispersed in: (h) different solvents and (i) DMSO solvent with varying volume ratios of water.
The possible emission mechanism of MD-GQDs with a solvent induced double emission behavior.

Fluorescence emission spectrum of MD-GQDs with different content of water in DMSO (a) and DMF (b). The images of MD-GQDs with different content of water in DMSO (c) and DMF (d) under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet light.

(a) English words and poems printed by ink-1 and ink-2, and their corresponding anti-counterfeiting processes. (b) Digital images of the printed QR code pattern and dual-encryption application. (c) Application of anti-counterfeiting technology in digital matrix coding.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of MD-GQDs (a) and the correlated double emission behavior (b). (c) The application of MD-GQDs in anti-counterfeiting.

head.ce:title: Intramolecular charge transfer mediated dual-emission in multi-doped graphene quantum dots for anti-counterfeiting via liquid secret key

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multiple emission GQDs have always been the focus of researchers due to the unique emission characteristics and high-level application capabilities. Nevertheless, the most of GQDs with double emissions are formed by different chemical reactions, and the realization of double emission by adjusting the solvent environment is rarely reported. Here, the multi-doped and double-peak emissive GQDs (MD-GQDs) have been synthesized via one pot hydrothermal synthesis and re-dispersed in solvents with different polarity. Systematic characterizations of optical property indicate that the solvent polarity related double-peak emission photoluminescence is ascribed to the existence of multi-emissive centers in MD-GQDs from aromatic CO bond and surface/edge state (C–N/C–F). Furthermore, the double emission fluorescent mechanism of MD-GQDs has been proposed due to the electrostatic interaction between MD-GQDs and solvent molecules, which induces intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect in MD-GQDs. Finally, based on the unique solvent-induced discoloration behavior of MD-GQDs, a multi-mode anti-counterfeiting application based on organic solution as the secret key with easy operation and high visual effect is developed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In all carbon-based materials, photoluminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have become a rising star in nanomaterials field since its discovery due to superior optical, stable chemical, electronic and biocompatible properties [1–3]. These comprehensive and unique performances make GQDs promising candidate for a vast variety of applications in optical and electrochemical equipment, catalysis and biomedical fields, etc. [4–6] So far, plentiful endeavors have focused on the fundamental and pivotal optical characteristic of GQDs. Among all optical adjustment methods, heteroatom doping is the most common and efficient method. Since the radius of the N atom is similar to the radius of the C atom, the N atom is easily incorporated into the carbon material skeleton [7,8]. The nitrogen atom can generate a new surface state energy level, causing a red shift of the emission wavelength [9]. In addition, fluorine atom is easily overlooked as a doped atom, which can obviously increase the polarization degree of GQDs [10,11]. Besides, the electron-rich phosphorus atom acted as an n-type donor to enhance the fluorescence properties of GQDs will contribute to the radiation transition pathway [12,13]. It is widely known that the diverse doping will bring lots of unexpected features to GQDs. However, there is no literature report on multi-doped GQDs with nitrogen, phosphorus and fluorine atoms. Thus, it is still a huge challenge for GQDs to be doped with various atoms and study the dependencies of these atoms.

Up to present, double-peak emissive GQDs have been successfully synthesized by tuning various precursor materials [14,15]. However, since the original mechanism of these double emissions is still ambiguous, these processes are unpredictable and the tunable emission peak seems to be difficult to establish. Preliminary studies have already demonstrated that the excited carbon core produces electronic excitons, which diffuse to the edge and surface states, where the electron recombination is carried out and finally the surface/edge state emission is realized [16,17]. In addition, the surface/edge emission of GQDs is easily vulnerable to the environmental stimulus (such as solvent polarity and pH, etc.) [18,19]. Therefore, this provides a rational strategy for creating double-peak emissive GQDs by simply adjusting the surrounding environment. Furthermore, some previous studies have shown that CO and/or C–N edge states of GQDs have their own “extrinsic” emission and the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process has a certain regulatory effect on the dual fluorescence emissions of the surface/edge state [20–22]. Although double-peak emissive GQDs have been reported in the literatures, the uncontrollable experiment conditions and fuzzy mechanism severely limit its development. Therefore, it is of great significance to achieve multiple emission centers by adjusting the distribution of electronic excitons on different surface functional groups of GQDs.

Lately, the counterfeiting of valuables such as confidential documents, currency and medicines has become a global concern as it severely disrupts socioeconomic and security [23,24]. Up till now, luminescence anti-counterfeiting is one of the research hotspots, due to relatively high photostability, pleochroism, easy to design, easy to see with the naked eye or read with certified tools such as digital cameras or spectrometers. Several luminescent security labels are usually produced by printing fluorescent dyes [25], inorganic quantum dots [26], rare earth materials and carbon-based quantum dots [27,28]. Among these fluorescent materials, carbon-based quantum dots stand out for their easy synthesis, environmental friendliness and low cost. Although dynamic anti-counterfeiting based on phosphorescent GQDs has been developed, transient dynamic changes make information capture difficult and prone to misjudgment [29]. To overcome the above disadvantages, developing noble luminescent GQDs with high convenience and reliable properties is highly demanded to prohibit a counterfeiting.

Motivated by the above issue, a novel multi-doped and double-peak emissive GQDs (MD-GQDs) are synthesized by using levofloxacin and phosphoric acid as precursor via a one-step hydrothermal method. The corresponding synthesis process is shown in Scheme 1 (a). After systematic characterization, the synthesized MD-GQDs successfully incorporate F, N and P atoms, which evolve with the various types of defects. Amusingly, by dispersing MD-GQDs in solvents with different polarity, the correlated transition from single-peak emission to double-peak emission is realized (Scheme 1(b)), which is rarely reported in other researches. This special phenomenon is attributed to the different abilities of CO defect state and surface/edge state (C–N/C–F) to capture electronic excitons under the influence of different solvent polarity. As the polarity of the solvent environment around GQDs decreases, the electrostatic interaction between the C–N/C–F bond and the solvent molecule is reduced, and the electronic excitons generated by the carbon core will diffuse to the CO bond and recombine with holes, which causing the second emission peak. Finally, we demonstrate the color-switch MD-GQDs as anti-counterfeiting ink for inkjet printing to prepare anti-counterfeiting labels, and using organic solvent as key to realize multi-mode anti-counterfeiting (Scheme 1(c)). This work not only provides insights into the formation and physical mechanisms of double-peak emissive MD-GQDs, but also paves the way for liquid-keyed anti-counterfeiting applications.

Levofloxacin, phosphoric acid, formamide, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and toluene are purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All of the reagents and materials are used as received without further purification. Deionized water produced by a Millipore water purification system (Milli-Q, Millipore) is used in all experiments.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of the MD-GQDs dissolved in different solvents are recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. A Hitachi FL7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer instrument is used to record the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and emission (PL) spectra. The fluorescence lifetime data are collected by the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system. The XRD pattern is collected using an XD-2X/M4600. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images are performed using a JEOL-2010 electron microscope. Raman spectrum (HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR 800) of MD-GQDs is obtained using an Ar+ laser with the wavelength of 532 nm. FT-IR spectra are obtained on a Nicolet Avatar 360 F T-IR spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is conducted on a Thermo ScientificTM K-AlphaTM + spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) operating at 100 W. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) measurements are taken by BRUKER AVANCE 400.

In a typical synthesis, 0.1 mmol of levofloxacin dissolved into 10 mL of phosphoric acid solution. After 15-min ultrasonic treatment, the obtained solution is sealed to a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 8 h. After that, a dark brown mixture containing green emissive MD-GQDs is obtained. For purification of MD-GQDs, the mixture solution passes through a 0.22 μm filter to remove the residue. Dilute the obtained solution with deionized water to keep the pH range of the solution at 6–9, then dialyzed against deionized water using a 500 Da cellulose membrane to remove the unreacted substances. After that, the powder of MD-GQDs is finally got by freeze-drying for future use.

The quantum yield (QY) of the MD-GQDs is measured according to the previous report [30]. Specially, rhodamine 6G (QY = 95% in ethanol, excitation wavelength 420 nm) is selected as the reference. To prevent self-absorption, the concentrations of MD-GQDs solutions should be adjusted to ensure all corresponding optical absorbance values are within 0–0.1. The QY of MD-GQDs is then calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
Q Y
X
=
Q Y
S T
×
(
K X
K
S T
)
×
(
η X
η
S T
)
2
where the “η” is the refractive index, “K” is the slope of integrated emission intensity against absorbance. The subscripts “ST” and “X” refer to the reference and target sample, respectively.

The morphologies of MD-GQDs are characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). As shown in Fig. 1 (a-b), well-dispersed nanoparticles can be observed from the TEM image with an average size about 7.10 ± 0.03 nm. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) shows a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite and reveals that the MD-GQDs contain graphite-like structures [31]. Furthermore, the AFM image indicates that the MD-GQDs are monodispersed with height of approximately 0.95 nm (Fig. 1(c)). Subsequently, the XRD pattern of MD-GQDs (Fig. S1(a)) has an apparent peak at approximately 22°, which is close to the diffraction peak of the graphite (002) crystal plane and the interlayer spacing is close to 0.35 nm, while the peak near 41° corresponds to characteristic (100) diffraction peak with d-spacing of 0.21 nm [18,32]. Moreover, the main mutual feature of the Raman spectrum (Fig. S1(b)) for MD-GQDs is the D band at 1356 cm−1 and the G band at around 1596 cm−1, whereas the 2D peak (∼2712 cm−1) is strongly suppressed. Besides, the calculated intensity ratio ID/IG is 0.51, which manifests evidence of high graphitization for MD-GQDs [33].

To further understand the role of atoms and functional groups, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are employed to investigate the structure of MD-GQDs. Particularly, MD-GQDs possessed a broad FT-IR absorption band at about 3200–3500 cm−1, 2933-2845 cm−1 and 2678-2557 cm−1 in Fig. 1(d), showing the presence of –OH/N–H, –CH2- and P–OH on the surface of MD-GQDs. The peaks of 1722 cm−1, 1614 cm−1, 1530 cm−1, 1454 cm−1 and 1311 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO, CN, CC, –NHCO– and C–N, respectively. More importantly, the obtained FT-IR spectrum exhibits significant absorption peaks of covalent C–F bonds at 1254 cm−1 and semi-ionic C–F bonds at 1082 cm−1, which indicate that highly polarized C–F bonds exist on the surface of MD-GQDs [10,34]. Additionally, phosphorus-related functional groups are detected, such as P–C (685 cm−1) and PO4 3− (551 cm−1) [35]. These FT-IR results show that there are vibrations of nitrogen, phosphorus and fluorine-related chemical bonds in MD-GQDs. Subsequently, the chemical composition of MD-GQDs is also verified through the survey of XPS studies. The full XPS spectrum presented in Fig. S2 shows five peaks at 284.14 eV, 400.56 eV, 532.62 eV, 132.66 eV and 687.51 eV, suggesting that the MD-GQDs consist of C, N, O, P and F elements, and the atomic ratios are calculated to be 63.3%, 11.1%, 13.2%, 8.1% and 4.3% (Table S1), respectively. In Fig. 1(e), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C 1s is separated into three peaks at 284.14 eV, 285.95 eV and 288.57 eV, which are ascribed to CC/C–O, C–N/C–O/C–P and C–F/OC–O groups [10]. As shown in Fig. 1(f), three resolved peaks in N 1s are determined at 400.04 eV, 400.93 eV and 402.10 eV, which are caused by pyridinic N, N–H and graphitic N. Likewise, the O 1s spectrum reveals the presence of three bands at 531.06 eV, 531.74 eV, 532.62 eV and 533.43 eV, which are derived from CO, C–O, P–O and OC–O units (Fig. 1(g)). Besides, two main peaks in high resolution P 2p spectrum (Fig. 1(h)) indicate the presence of P–O/P–C bond (132.52 eV) and phosphate group (133.54 eV). Furthermore, the F 1s band in Fig. 1(i) contains two peaks at 684.89 eV for semi-ionic C–F and 687.54 eV for covalent C–F [34]. Recently, NMR spectroscopy has been proposed as an efficient tool for the characterization of the chemical structure of organic molecules. Thus, NMR spectra (13C, 1H, 19F and 31P) are employed to provide a more detailed demonstration of the structure of MD-GQDs (Fig. S3). Based on the above results, the existence of these bonds further indicates that N, P and F atoms are effectively doped into the structure of MD-GQDs. These results indicate that there are abundant functional groups on the surface of MD-GQDs, which ensure the excellent optical properties.

The UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of the as-prepared MD-GQDs aqueous solution are examined to evaluate their optical properties. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), absorption spectrum exhibits five peaks at 218 nm, 248 nm, 276 nm, 346 nm and 396 nm. Typically, MD-GQDs show absorption bands at 218 nm, 248 nm and 276 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition and the other absorption bands at 346 nm and 396 nm can be assigned to the n-π* transition. Furthermore, the PL spectrum of the MD-GQDs shows the maximum emission peak at 513 nm under the maximum excitation (393 nm). It is worth noting that the small overlap between the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and the emission spectrum of MD-GQDs indicates their small self-absorption, which is of great benefit to the efficient fluorescence emission of MD-GQDs. The aqueous solution of MD-GQDs shows a faint brown-yellow under sunlight, while it shows bright green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation. Fig. 2(b) represents the PL emission under different excitation wavelengths, showing a stable green emission. Similarly, two-dimensional spectral scanning (Fig. 2(c)) for MD-GQDs demonstrates a single fluorescence emission center (393 nm, 513 nm). Besides, the QY of the MD-GQDs aqueous solution is calculated to be 80.6%. In addition, the stability of the MD-GQDs (pH, ionic strength and storage time) should be evaluated for their application. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the PL intensity and emission wavelength of our synthesized MD-GQDs exhibit good stability at a wide pH range from 1 to 11. However, when pH is above 11, the fluorescence intensity of MD-GQDs decreases rapidly and the emission peak is accompanied by obvious blue shift. This can be explained by the deprotonation of P- and N-related functional groups of MD-GQDs [36]. Besides, as shown in Fig. 2(e), the fluorescence intensity of MD-GQDs keeps almost unchanged under the concentration of NaCl up to 2 mol/L, and the maximum fluorescence emission peak remained steady. Fig. 2(f) shows the fluorescence intensity decreases mildly with increasing storage time, which implies that the MD-GQDs possess great storage stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intramolecular charge transfer mediated dual-emission in MD-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To thoroughly investigate the luminescent properties, a series of optical properties of MD-GQDs have also been explored in other solvent environments (formamide, ethanol, DMSO, DMF and toluene). Firstly, two-dimensional fluorescence spectra of MD-GQDs solution with different solvents are collected and shown in Fig. 3 (a-f). Obviously, a significant change is observed for ethanol, DMSO, DMF and toluene solvents, in which a new fluorescence emission center appears. For the MD-GQDs in water (ET N = 1) and formamide (ET N = 0.77) solvents, the optimum emission center of MD-GQDs remains unchanged at 513 nm with an excitation-independent characteristic. When MD-GQDs is dispersed in ethanol (ET N = 0.654), the fluorescence spectrum shows an overlapping envelope peak containing double fluorescence centers (FC) (FC-1 at 498 nm, FC-2 at 437 nm), which indicates that the fluorescence spectrum of MD-GQDs has undergone a transition between the single- and double-peak emission. On the other hand, with the decrease of the solvent polarity, the MD-GQDs presents magic double peaks in three solvents, i.e. DMSO (EN T = 0.46, FC-1 at 495 nm, FC-2 at 385 nm), DMF (EN T = 0.386, FC-1 at 492 nm, FC-2 at 382 nm) and toluene (EN T = 0.1, FC-1 at 485 nm, FC-2 at 327 nm), where the FC-1 represents the intrinsic fluorescence emission center of MD-GQDs, and FC-2 represents the luminescence center induced by solvents. This solvent-dependent fluorescence behavior of MD-GQDs may be dominated by different types of electron transitions between the carbon core basal plane and different functional groups [37–39].

In order to exclude that FC-2 is caused by the fluorescence of the solvent itself, the emission spectra of six solvents are scanned at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, as shown in Fig. S4. Water, ethanol, DMSO and DMF solvents show no emission peak, while formamide solvent shows a weak peak packet at 417–440 nm, indicating that the tails near 420 nm of the MD-GQDs/formamide solution are caused by the solvent emission peak. For toluene solvent, we found that the MD-GQDs/toluene solution has two emission peaks at 411 nm and 433 nm, which correspond to the emission peaks at 413 nm and 433 nm of the toluene solvent. Thus, the two emission peaks are caused by solvent itself. But it is worth noting that the MD-GQDs/toluene solution shows a shoulder peak at 485 nm (Fig. S5). Therefore, the analytical results demonstrate that the emergence of FC-2 may originate from solvent-induced redistribution of electrons in MD-GQDs. In addition, the excitation-emission fluorescence spectra of MD-GQDs in different solvents are recorded. As show in Fig. 3(g-l), the green area represents FC-1 of MD-GQDs, and the purple area represents FC-2. Obviously, in water and formamide solutions, the fluorescence spectra of MD-GQDs exhibit conventional single emission center and stable excitation-independent emission behavior in green area. For other solvents, MD-GQDs reveal double-peak emission centers in green and purple areas. Note that FC-1 also shows an excitation-independent characteristic with the increase of excitation wavelength. Besides, both of the FC-1 and FC-2 exhibit a solvent-dependent emission behavior, which further confirms that MD-GQDs have different emission states in these four solvents. This phenomenon is due to the hydrogen bonding or dipole-dipole interaction between the surface functional groups of MD-GQDs (such as nitrogen-related functional groups and C–F bonds) and solvent molecules, thus reducing the energy level gap and causing a red shift, which is consistent with the solvatochromism phenomenon in other literature [10,39,40]. As shown in Fig. S6, the optimal excitation wavelength for FC-1 of MD-GQDs in six solvents all shows an obvious peak near 400 nm, which is accordance with their absorption band at 396 nm. It is worth noting that the excitonic absorption band at 396 nm is attributed to the n-π* transitions of surface/edge band bonds (C–N/C–F) [34,41,42]. Thus, the FC-1 should be resulted from the surface or edge state (C–N/C–F). Meanwhile, the optimal excitation wavelength for FC-2 of MD-GQDs in four solvents has a large overlap with the absorption peak at 346 nm in the corresponding UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S7), which is attributed to the n-π* transitions of CO bands [43]. Thus, it is inferred that the appearance of FC-2 is related to the electronic transitions within different emission centers of MD-GQDs (CO, C–N/C–F).

Solvent-dependent dual emission centers of MD-GQDs are observed in emission spectrum, which is notably rare for single-component luminescent materials. To explore the causes of these phenomena for MD-GQDs, further experiments are carried out. As shown in Fig. 4 (a-c) and Fig. S8, three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectra are performed under different volume ratios between water and polar solvents (DMSO, DMF and ethanol). With the DMSO content in water gradually increases, the MD-GQDs solution undergoes a clear fluorescence center transition from single to double emissions. Correspondingly, the fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelengths are also displayed in Fig. S9. Further evidence for the similar solvent-indicating colorimetric property is that the evolution of fluorescence center for MD-GQDs in DMF and ethanol solvents is accordance with the phenomenon of DMSO, as shown in Figs. S10–S13. The above results indicate that the fluorescence center transition process of MD-GQDs can be easily controlled by adjusting the ratio between two solvents, and meanwhile, it also implies that there is a complex interaction between MD-GQDs and solvent molecules, which leads to this magical phenomenon. In addition to the fluorescence center responsive to the solvent, the distinct UV–Vis absorption spectra of MD-GQDs in mixed solutions with different ratios of DMSO to water are measured, and the results are displayed in Fig. 4(d). It can be seen that three types of absorption peaks (purple area represents carbon core; blue area represents aromatic structures (CO bands); green area represents surface/edge state (C–N/C–F)) change with the gradual increase of DMSO content in water. As a result, the absorption peaks produced by the carbon core show notable change, which indicate the electronic state in carbon core is also affected by the solvent around. At the same time, as shown in Fig. 4(e), there is no obvious shift both of the CO absorption peak and C–N/C–F absorption peak, while the former peaks gradually strengthen, and the latter peaks gradually weaken with the increase of DMSO content. Similar transitions of absorption peak of MD-GQDs in water with different content of DMF or ethanol are also obtained in Fig. S14. Thus, it is reasonable to deduce that the solvent-dependent PL behaviors could be explained by two aspects; one possible reason is the electrostatic interaction between MD-GQDs and solvent molecules because of the hydrogen bond or dipole-dipole interaction [44,45]; another possible reason is the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) from FC-1 to FC-2 [20,46,47]. Previous studies have shown that in aprotic solvents (DMF, DMSO and toluene), the electrostatic interaction between GQDs and solvent molecules is usually dipole-dipole interaction, while hydrogen bond interaction occurs in protonic solvents (water, formamide and ethanol) [37]. The polar capacity of the solvent can be effectively described by the solvent polarity parameter (ET N), which is a normalized parameter describing the overall solvation capability of solvents as a combination of nonspecific (dipole-dipole) and specific (hydrogen bond) interactions [48]. With the increase of solvent polarity, both of FC-1 and FC-2 have a certain red-shift from toluene to water (Fig. 4(f)), which is consistent with the characteristic of most fluorophores that show solvatochromism-character. More importantly, the solvent polarity has a significant impact on FC-2, and the spectral transition between FC-1 and FC-2 is ascribed to the solvent polarity-induced ICT process (Fig. 4(g)), which induces the formation of dual peak emissions and the bandgap changes of MD-GQDs in different solvents. According to reports, the hydrogen bond and dipole interaction can affect the state of electronic transition movement and electron cloud density distribution in GQDs [39,40,44]. Therefore, with the decrease of the solvent polarity, the components of the ICT effect in the mixed excited state increase correspondingly.

To provide a more rigorous confirmation of ICT process, the time-resolved PL spectrum of MD-GQDs is measured to further explore the underlying mechanism (Fig. 4(h-i)). The results show that all solvents can be fitted by a dual-exponential formula:
(2)
I
( t )
=
B 1
exp
(
− t / τ
1
)
+
B 2
exp
(
− t / τ
2
)
where “B1” and “B2” are the pre-exponential factors and “τ1” and “τ2” are the fitted lifetimes, respectively. All fitting equation involves a short-lived component τ1 and a long-lived component τ2. As the solvent polarity increases (Fig. 4(h)), the two components of PL lifetime show continuous prolongation which are in accordance to the PL emission results. From highly polar solvent (water) to low polarity solvent (toluene), the short-lived component τ1 weight increases from 3% to 42%, while the long-lived component τ2 weight decreases from 97% to 58%. The corresponding data is summarized in Table S2. Moreover, we also test the lifetime decay curve of MD-GQDs dispersed in DMSO solvent with varying volume ratios of water (from 0 to 100%) (Fig. 4(i) and Table S3). Predictably, with the gradual decrease of the water content in DMSO, the weight of short lifetime increases gradually from 3% to 31%, while the weight of long lifetime decreases from 97% to 69%. Based on the above results, it is believed that the generation of additional FC-2 peak is ascribed to ICT effect, which lessens the electron transfer to the C–N/C–F groups in the MD-GQDs with the decrease of solvent polarity.

The fluorescence kinetic process of MD-GQDs with double-peak emissions consists of three steps: MD-GQDs absorb 365 nm UV light to reach the excited singlet state. Then, some of the excited electrons transfer energy to the C–N/C–F groups, which promote the green fluorescence emission. Furthermore, it has been reported that the electron-rich phosphorus atom acts as an n-type donor to enhance the fluorescence properties of the MD-GQDs and contribute to the radiation transition pathway [12]. The versatile optical properties of MD-GQDs can be rationally explained in terms of the proposed model in Fig. 5 . These nitrogen and fluorine-related defect states can induce electrostatic interactions with surrounding solvent molecules (such as hydrogen bonds or dipole-dipole interactions) and attract a large number of electrons from the carbon core region, resulting in green fluorescence emission (FC-1) [49,50]. Nevertheless, as the polarity of the solvent around MD-GQDs decreases, the electrostatic interaction between MD-GQDs and solvent molecules weakens, and more excited electrons transfer energy to the CO groups by ICT effect. This implicit edge state captures more electrons and promotes the radiation recombination of electron-hole pairs, which effectively improve the fluorescence intensity and provide a novel route for the blue fluorescence emission (FC-2). Therefore, in low-polarity solvents, MD-GQDs exhibit dual-emission behavior.

Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of MD-GQDs in DMSO and DMF with different water volumes are systematically investigated. As presented in Fig. 6 (a), as the volume content of water in DMSO increases, FC-1 gradually redshifts while FC-2 remains stable, and finally the double peaks merge into a single peak emission. The overall fluorescence intensity of both peaks is increasing, with increasing water content. Notably, the spectral variation of the solution covers the blue, cyan and green ranges. Besides, the increased water content in DMF also induces similar spectrum changes, and similarly covers the blue, cyan and green ranges (Fig. 6 (b)). Attractively, the photographs in Fig. 6(c) show the color changes of the solution from dark blue to cyan and finally fluoresces bright green with increasing water content in DMSO (0–100%) under 365 nm UV irradiation, while Fig. 6(d) exhibits a color transition from green to blue and finally to green as increasing water content in DMF (0–100%). This indicates that the synthesized MD-GQDs have solvent-dependent color-changing behavior.

Benefiting from the color-switch behavior of MD-GQDs under unique conditions, a convenient anti-counterfeiting technology based on liquid secret key is actualized by using MD-GQDs as printing ink. Fig. 7 (a) shows the detailed information anti-counterfeiting process, where ink-1 and ink-2 represent green fluorescent dye and MD-GQDs. In addition, the DMSO solution worked as liquid secret key provides a function of ink fluorescence color shift that can be used to verify the authenticity of the item. In the first step, the password and interference information are printed on non-luminescent commercial paper using two inks of ink-1 and ink-2, respectively. Under irradiation with a 365 nm UV light, the printed ink shows a single green fluorescence and the password is indistinguishable from the interference information. Then, when the paper containing anti-counterfeiting information is soaked by DMSO solution, it is obviously observed that the standing initial of the words remains the originally green fluorescence under UV light. On the contrary, the fluorescence of the position printed by ink-2 changes to dark blue. Similarly, the hiding of key information in the poetry has also actualized by using ink-1 and ink-2 as printing inks.

In addition, the quick response code (QR code) has been widely used in daily life and business establishments. However, the common commercial QR code brings down the anti-counterfeiting effect with a large number of duplicates. To address this issue, an advanced dual-encryption utilization QR code is realized by printing with ink-1 and ink-3 that is formed by mixing ink-1 and ink-2 with the same volume. Remarkably, ink-3 prints actual QR code information, while ink-1 acts as an interference module. As depicted in Fig. 7(b), the printed paper presents a single color under the UV light source, and the real encrypted area is indiscernible even after injection of DMSO solution. And then, it is surprising that the real encrypted area stands out from the color-indistinguishable QR code by additional injection of water in the print area. Subsequently, to demonstrate the diversity of our anti-counterfeiting technology, a digital password “0649” is encoded by four separate pixel matrices (5 × 10) on non-luminous commercial paper by printing with ink-1 and ink-3. As demonstrated in Fig. 7(c), the color of the password coding area changes from green to blue and shows the data password “0649” after the matrix is sprayed with a mixed solution of DMSO and water (1:1 vol ratio). Therefore, this distinctive liquid-based anti-counterfeiting method may provide an opportunity for commercial, pharmaceutical, military secrets and hopefully many others.

In conclusion, by using levofloxacin and phosphoric acid as precursors, MD-GQDs doped with nitrogen, phosphorus and fluorine have been synthesized via one pot hydrothermal synthesis. The MD-GQDs exhibit unique optical property from a single emission center to dual emission centers, which can be adjusted by the polarity strength of the surrounding solvent. Through experiments and data analysis, we verify that FC-1 and FC-2 are the fluorescent luminescence centers produced by the electronic transition between the surface/edge state (C–N/C–F) and the aromatic structure CO bonds, respectively. With the decrease of solvent polarity from water to toluene, the ICT effect causes more excited electrons to transfer energy from C–N/C–F to CO, changing the fluorescence of MD-GQDs with convertible dual emission centers. Benefiting from the unique color-changing performance, the obtained MD-GQDs exhibit a good potential in liquid-based dual-encryption anti-counterfeiting. This work may broaden and open a gateway to the development of multicolor luminescence materials in vital information transmission and luminous safety labels.

Changxing Wang: analyzed experimental results and wrote the manuscript. Xiameng Li: conducted the experiments. Tingting Li: involved in analyzing experimental results. Yiru Wang: assisted in analyzing experimental results and software data processing. Guoqiang Guo: assisted in Investigation and Formal analysis. Siyuan Tang: assisted in Investigation and Formal analysis. Chenjie Gu: assisted in reviewing the manuscript. Da Chen: revised the manuscript and provided Funding acquisition. All authors read and contributed to the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084), and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.
